## Valorant - a Valiant Idea

One does not take on the titan that is Counter-Strike lightly but this is exactly what Riot Games is trying to do in their recent free-to-play release, Valorant. With its single-life bomb planting/defusing game mode, an equipment buying system, and tactical shooting mechanics, you might immediately try to call this a Counter-Strike clone... but, you'd be wrong.

Valorant is an excellent game in its own right and it definitely doesn't deserve to be branded as a Counter-Strike knock off. While many of the mechanics and other game aspects might feel similar, Valorant has a lot that makes it unique. The biggest difference is Valorant's ability system which gives players a variety of special powers based on the character (agent) they choose. Players get one free ability per agent and then have the option to purchase two other tactical abilities during the buy phase. Additionally, each agent has an ultimate ability which is earned by performing key actions (eliminating players, planting or defusing the spike, and dying) as well as by collecting black orbs which appear in specific locations across the map. While the hip-fire aiming feels similar to Counter-Strike, another big difference that Valorant offers is the ability to aim down sights with most weapons, which may also change the weapon's stats.

The gameplay, as a whole, is great with solid mechanics that have been proven to work time and time again. The weapons are well-balanced and no agent seems to stand head and shoulders above the others. This is expected from a company with years of experience in maintaining balance in a game starring more than a hundred vastly different characters (League of Legends). As time progresses, we can expect Riot Games to take an active approach to ensuring all agents fulfil a useful role in the game.

One notable aspect of Valorant is its excellent netcode. Even when playing on EU West servers all the way from South Africa, the game is smooth and still feels competitive. Riot Games has mentioned that they would like Valorant to be a global game but, with South Africa still lacking servers for League of Legends, it's uncertain if we will see local servers in the near future. This may definitely prove to be detrimental to the popularity of the game in South Africa where Counter-Strike has had local servers for years.

There have been many people advocating for and against Valorant's art style which is cartoon-esque. I must say that I'm a fan of it but I understand why some may prefer more of a realistic look. The art style does assist with player experience - all objects are clearly defined and there is no doubt as to whether you're behind cover or not. As an added plus, the map design is excellent with a clear focus on the competitive aspects. The one-way teleporters on certain maps make for a nice dynamic and ensure that choke points do not become unbreakable.

From all of the above we can see that Valorant is geared towards supporting competitive play from the ground up which is further reinforced by the inclusion of a ranked-play mode, dubbed Rated queue, as well as commitment to the future of the game in esports. This commitment includes 'forming partnerships with players, content creators, tournament organizers, and developers — unlocking them to help us [Riot Games] to build this ecosystem'.

This being said, Riot Games has come under fire for the methods they are using to maintain the competitive integrity of their game. While just about everyone detests cheaters, Valorant's implementation of a kernel-level anticheat driver (called Vanguard) is questionable. Vanguard starts running when your computer boots, a privilege which is generally reserved for crucial programs such as your antivirus. While Riot Games acknowledged the complaints, they released an announcement stating that they still believe this is the best method to curb cheating in their new game.

Another issue is the player base of the game. Unfortunately, toxic communities have become rife in online games and Valorant is no exception. There have already been various reports of players encountering people who are behaving obnoxiously. This seems to be part and parcel of the online experience these days but it's good to see that the developers are 'looking into long-term solutions' to solve this problem whilst still promoting an environment that is conducive to teamwork.

As I mentioned in the opening paragraph, Valorant is free to play which is fantastic news for newcomers who want to try it out without spending anything. For the players who are more invested, there is the option to purchase weapon skins directly as well as a battle pass with free and paid-for tiers. One point to note about the battle pass is that, unlike other games, players are unable to earn in-game currency from leveling up the premium version of the pass. This is an interesting decision from Riot Games and may dissuade players from paying for the battle pass since there is no chance of earning the next one by playing.
 
Having played the beta and the full release of the game, I can say that not much has changed but, to be honest, not much needed to. It's a great game that is ticking all the right boxes with any issues being addressed rapidly by a dedicated support team. All in all, Valorant is a valiant idea out of Riot Games but only time will tell if it has what it takes to beat the competition and claim the crown of the most popular first-person shooter on PC.

_Jason Parry_
